evidence: Package layout — api, config, exploration, indexer/blacklab, model, model/exploration, query, utils — maps directly to domain capabilities; each name states its role without historical accident.

API layer decomposes into five focused handler classes (SketchHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, ConcordanceHandlers, CorpusQueryHandlers, VisualizationHandlers), all package-private under WordSketchApiServer, giving a small and predictable public surface.
Config package cleanly separates data (GrammarConfig), I/O (GrammarConfigLoader), and serialization (GrammarConfigSerializer) — three distinct responsibilities, three classes.
model/package-info.java explicitly documents the two-tier model split (pure DTOs vs immutable classes with derived accessors), making ownership legible to a new engineer.

RadialPlot.java is a visualization-computation class (SVG layout maths, spiral constants) placed inside the api package alongside HTTP handlers; a developer tracing rendering logic would not expect to find it there, and it blurs the api package's stated responsibility ('HTTP request handlers, routing utilities, and response assembly').

model/ root holds QueryResults, PosGroup, RelationType while model/exploration/ holds 10+ types; there is no sibling sub-package for word-sketch results, creating a mild structural asymmetry that a new engineer must reason about (why are some models flat, others sub-packaged?).
pom.xml declares 5 production dependencies: blacklab-engine, blacklab-query-parser, jackson-databind, jackson-core, slf4j-api, logback-classic, jspecify — each is actively used.

jackson-core (com.fasterxml.jackson.core:jackson-core) is declared explicitly alongside jackson-databind at the same version (2.17.2 via shared property), but jackson-core is already a mandatory transitive dependency of jackson-databind. Direct uses (JsonProcessingException in CorpusQueryHandlers.java, VisualizationHandlers.java, GrammarConfigLoader.java) are valid but do not require the explicit pom entry.
No duplicate JSON libraries: only Jackson is used for JSON serialisation/deserialisation across the entire codebase.
SLF4J API + Logback classic is the standard API/implementation split; no mixing of log4j, JUL or commons-logging observed.
jspecify is used consistently in 116 import locations across query, model, config, and indexer packages — clearly earning its place despite being annotation-only.

blacklab-query-parser is a genuinely used separate artifact: ContextualQueryLanguageParser in BlackLabQueryExecutor.java and CorpusQueryLanguageParser (inline import) in CollocateQueryHelper.java both resolve from this artifact.

blacklab-engine and blacklab-query-parser at version 4.0.0 are NOT published to Maven Central (latest Central release is 3.0.1); they require a manual install step (install-blacklab.sh/ps1) documented in the pom and a github-raw fallback profile — this is a procurement friction issue, not a version conflict.

CollocateQueryHelper.buildAndRankCollocates() and scoreHits() each independently implement an identical 'pre-fetch corpus frequency for all unique collocates into a local HashMap' loop — same pattern duplicated within the same class across two private methods.

SemanticFieldExplorer.buildNounToCollocatesMap() issues up to 20+ sequential executor.executeCollocations() I/O calls in a for-loop over seedCollocateScores.keySet() with no comment about the fan-out cost, no time budget, and no early-exit; the N-sequential-IO pattern is invisible at the call site.

BlackLabQueryExecutor.collectFrequenciesAndPosFromGroups() accepts a Function<String,String> lemma extractor, but the three call sites pass inconsistent fallback policies: one uses extractLastLemma, another extractLemmaWithFallback (which itself has a plain-text fallback), and a third uses a composed two-tier lambda (extractCollocateFromXmlByPosition then extractPlainTextTokenAt). The shared method abstraction obscures different per-caller extraction semantics.

Guard clauses and early returns are used consistently across all handlers and explorers (executeCollocations, executeDependencyPattern, exploreByPattern, HttpApiUtils.requireParam, all BlackLabSnippetParser methods).

Phase decomposition is clean: CollocateQueryHelper.executeBcqlQuery separates collectHits / scoreHits phases with a named HitRecord bridging record; MultiSeedExplorer decomposes into fetchCollocatesPerSeed / identifyCommonCollocates / aggregateCollocateStats / buildDiscoveredNouns / buildCoreCollocates — each a single-purpose private method.

Private record types (CollocateSearch, HitRecord, AggregatedCollocateStats, SeedCollocateData, BcqlRequest, CommonExploreParams, ExploreParameterExtras, ExploreVariantFields) are used throughout to give local carrier data named types rather than raw parallel collections.

State mutation is bounded and explicit: freqMap accumulation happens locally in collectHits with a Map passed by reference, accumulator semantics are visible in the method signature; no shared mutable state between phases.
BlackLabSnippetParser applies a consistent null-guard strategy — every method opens with 'if (xml == null || xml.isEmpty()) return ...' — making the defensive surface uniform and readable.

ExploreResponseAssembler is a clean single-purpose boundary translator: all model-to-JSON translation for exploration responses lives there, keeping ExplorationResult/ComparisonResult free of presentation concerns.
ExplorationService interface provides a stable, explicit seam between HTTP handlers and the exploration algorithm layer; ExplorationHandlers delegates cleanly without back-and-forth calls.
GrammarConfigLoader + GrammarConfig + GrammarConfigSerializer cleanly partition I/O, data, and presentation: each layer has one concern and depends only inward.
QueryExecutor default methods (executeSurfacePatternForRelation, executeBcqlQueryForRelation) centralise pattern-construction logic, keeping HTTP handlers free of RelationPatternBuilder calls.
HttpApiUtils.wrapWithErrorHandling provides uniform error propagation across all handler boundaries (IllegalArgumentException→400, IOException→500), with a single mapping point.

ConcordanceHandlers.handleConcordanceExamples calls grammarConfig.relation(req.relation()) without going through RelationUtils.resolveRelationAlias, so aliases like 'adj_predicate' silently fail to resolve there while working in ExplorationHandlers.resolveRelationConfig, which calls resolveRelationAlias before the same grammarConfig.relation() lookup.

ComparisonResult.fullyShared(), partiallyShared(), specific() re-implement filter predicates (presentInCount == nouns.size(), >= 2 and < size, == 1) that are already encapsulated in CollocateProfile.isFullyShared()/isPartiallyShared()/isSpecific(); summaryCounts() correctly delegates to sharingCategory() but the streaming accessors do not.

ConcordanceHandlers.toResultMap is a trivial one-liner that adds zero translation and exists only to alias ExploreResponseAssembler.collocateToExampleMap — adds navigational indirection with no benefit.
Guard clauses throughout are accompanied by explanatory comments (e.g. extractCollocateFromXmlByPosition position<1 note, extractCollocateLemma collocatePos>0 check) making intent clear

In executeBcqlQuery / collectHits, collocatePos == -1 is handled via explicit > 0 guard in extractCollocateLemma with fallback to extractLastLemma; in executeSurfacePattern, the same -1 return passes directly to extractCollocateFromXmlByPosition and extractPlainTextTokenAt, both of which return null for position < 1, silently producing empty results with no logging

The comment in BlackLabSnippetParser.extractCollocateFromXmlByPosition claims BcqlPatternUtils.findLabelTokenIndex() 'returns >= 1', but CqlUtils.findLabelTokenIndex actually returns -1 on failure, creating a false contract that could mislead future callers

VisualizationHandlers.RenderMode.isValid hardcodes the literal string 'SIGNED' instead of using enum values/valueOf; a new enum constant would silently bypass validation without any compile-time signal
Boolean/branching logic in SemanticFieldExplorer (filterByMinShared, identifyCoreCollocates), MultiSeedExplorer, and CollocateProfileComparator is clear and each phase is well-named
Main.parseIntParam / parseDoubleParam use System.exit(1) followed by throw new AssertionError('unreachable') — standard Java idiom, comment present, not a clarity defect
No dead code paths after unconditional returns, no always-true/false conditions, no identical if/else branches were found across the reviewed files

JSpecify @NonNull/@Nullable annotations used consistently on QueryExecutor interface, HttpApiUtils public methods, BlackLabSnippetParser return types, MultiSeedExplorer parameters, and ExplorationService interface
Sealed ConcordanceResult interface with 'permits QueryResults.SnippetResult, QueryResults.CollocateResult' enables exhaustive type narrowing at call sites
Domain layer uses strongly-typed records (WordSketchResult, CollocateResult, ExplorationResult, ComparisonResult) rather than untyped maps — type discipline is strong below the HTTP layer
RelationType and RelationEdgeType enums used as typed parameters throughout query dispatch and graph serialization paths

VisualizationHandlers defines a private RenderMode enum but RenderMode.isValid(String) hard-codes the literal "SIGNED".equalsIgnoreCase(value) instead of iterating enum values — the enum is defined but not leveraged for validation; also passes mode as raw String to RadialPlot.renderFromItems instead of converting to RenderMode

API response layer uses pervasive Map<String, Object> (ExploreResponseAssembler, ExplorationHandlers, SketchHandlers, CorpusQueryHandlers) — the response shape contract is invisible at compile time, though this is a deliberate serialization-boundary pattern
RelationConfig.relationType() is @Nullable, correctly annotated, but callers in SketchHandlers must guard against null; this is handled correctly via null-checks before rt.name()

CollocateQueryHelper constructor accepts @NonNull BlackLabIndex but also has a no-arg constructor for tests that sets index to @Nullable — the @Nullable field is then accessed via Objects.requireNonNull at the call sites, maintaining safety
Seven clearly-scoped top-level packages: api, config, exploration, indexer, model, query, utils — each with a package-info.java description

model/exploration/ sub-package cleanly separates 11 exploration-specific DTOs (CollocateProfile, Edge, ExplorationResult, etc.) from general model types (PosGroup, QueryResults, RelationType) in model/
api/ package-info.java states 'HTTP request handlers, routing utilities, and response assembly' but RadialPlot.java — a 200-line SVG rendering engine — lives there as a package-private class
GrammarConfigSerializer.java in config/ introduces a Jackson ObjectMapper dependency; the class Javadoc says it serves 'API responses and diagnostic output' yet it lives in the config loading package
config/ package-info.java lists 'serialization' as one of its concerns, showing the mismatch is acknowledged but accepted rather than resolved

Package-private visibility is used consistently for internals: RadialPlot, MultiSeedExplorer, CollocateProfileComparator, ExplorationHandlers, SketchHandlers, etc. keep concerns contained within their packages

ExplorationService interface lives in exploration/ where its implementation resides; callers in api/ import it from exploration/ — standard DI wiring but the interface is the API seam, not an exploration-layer internal
All 10+ HTTP handlers use identical error handling via HttpApiUtils.wrapWithErrorHandling, mapping IllegalArgumentException→400, RequestEntityTooLargeException→413, and generic Exception→500
Shared param helpers (requireParam, parseIntParam, parseDoubleParam) are used uniformly across all handlers — no raw Integer.parseInt at call sites
ExplorationService defines typed options objects (SingleSeedExplorationOptions, ExplorationOptions, FetchExamplesOptions) keeping all three exploration methods to 3 parameters

buildSharedExploreResponse unifies the JSON envelope structure across explore, explore-multi, and compare endpoints with ExploreParameterExtras/ExploreVariantFields distinguishing what goes in 'parameters' vs top level

populateComparisonResponse emits 'adjectives' and 'adjectives_count' keys while populateExploreResponse emits 'seed_collocates'/'discovered_nouns'/'core_collocates' — semantically different layouts for the same conceptual operation (finding shared collocates) on related endpoints
ExplorationService.fetchExamples signature uses 'headword' for the second parameter, but every HTTP handler and HTTP parameter name uses 'seed' — the interface-to-HTTP vocabulary is inconsistent

handleSemanticFieldExamples response includes 'total_results' field; handleConcordanceExamples response includes 'fallback' field — these are structurally parallel endpoints (both return concordance examples for seed+collocate) but their response shapes diverge

QueryExecutor exposes four overlapping query-execution methods: executeCollocations, executeCqlQuery, executeBcqlQuery, executeSurfacePattern — distinctions are documented but the surface requires callers to know which variant to use for each scenario
Domain vocabulary is consistently applied throughout: 'collocate', 'logDice', 'bcql', 'lemma', 'seed', 'sketch', 'deprel' appear across all layers with stable meaning.

ExplorationHandlers has two opaque private record types — ExploreParameterExtras and ExploreVariantFields — whose names do not communicate that one lands inside the 'parameters' sub-map and the other at the top-level response envelope; the developer must read the Javadoc to understand the structural difference.

buildSharedExploreResponse in ExplorationHandlers is ambiguous: 'shared' reads as the domain concept (shared collocates across seeds) when it actually means 'shared across three handler methods' (an implementation reuse concept); the codebase heavily uses 'shared' as a domain term (isFullyShared, partiallyShared, FULLY_SHARED), compounding the confusion.

CollocateQueryHelper exposes executeCollocateSearch, executeCollocateSearchWithContent, and executeCollocateSearchImpl — the 'Impl' suffix signals naming breakdown: the impl/non-impl distinction (whether to store hits for content retrieval) belongs in the name itself, not in a vestigial 'Impl' suffix.

The HTTP query parameter 'nouns_per' (ExplorationHandlers line 262) is bound to a Java variable named 'collocatesPerSeed' — these name different things: 'nouns_per' implies nouns-per-adjective while 'collocatesPerSeed' implies collocates-per-seed, which is the actual semantics; a developer reading the API doc alongside the code will be confused.

Inconsistent abbreviation: ConcordanceHandlers uses 'rel' (boolean fallback = rel.isEmpty()), SketchHandlers uses 'rel' (Map relData = buildSurfaceRelationEntry(rel, ...)), while CorpusQueryHandlers uses 'relation' — these name the same concept and appear at the same lexical scope level.

SketchHandlers has a private static toMap-equivalent named formatWordSketchResult; ConcordanceHandlers has toResultMap; ExploreResponseAssembler has collocateToFullResultMap and collocateToExampleMap — four methods that convert result objects to JSON-ready maps use four different naming conventions.

All BlackLabConllUIndexerTest tests are annotated @Disabled with a comment stating 'Requires conllu-sentences.blf.yaml format config in the project root — not distributed with source'; the indexing pipeline (CoNLL-U → BlackLab on-disk index) never runs in CI.

BlackLabQueryExecutorIntegrationTest is annotated @Disabled('Requires a live BlackLab index — set CONCEPT_SKETCH_TEST_INDEX env var and remove @Disabled to run'); all live-query execution paths are excluded from automated builds.
BlackLabQueryExecutorTest.LiveIndex nested class is also @Disabled, so real Lucene/BlackLab search behaviour (pattern execution, BCQL compilation, result ranking) is never verified automatically.

Handler tests (ExplorationHandlersTest, SketchHandlersTest, ConcordanceHandlersTest, CorpusQueryHandlersTest) cover both happy-path 200 responses and validation/negative paths (400, 413) consistently.
SemanticFieldExplorerTest, MultiSeedExplorerTest, CollocateProfileComparatorTest, CollocateQueryHelperTest all use well-structured StubQueryExecutor-backed unit tests with behavioural assertions.
WordSketchApiServerTest starts a real HTTP server on an ephemeral port and verifies routing, lifecycle, and teardown — a genuine integration test at the API boundary.
SemanticFieldExplorerTest.exploreMultiSeed_returnsNonNullResult only calls assertNotNull(result) — a trivially weak assertion that passes even if the result object is populated incorrectly.
All type names use PascalCase, all method/field names use camelCase, all constants use UPPER_SNAKE_CASE — no naming drift found anywhere in the codebase
Every package has a package-info.java documenting its boundaries; api/, config/, exploration/, model/, query/, utils/ all have clear, distinct mandates
model/package-info.java explicitly documents the intentional record-vs-class split with rationale, so the mixed type strategy is not an outlier

RadialPlot lives in api/ but is a pure SVG layout/rendering engine with no HTTP concern; api/package-info.java states the package is for 'HTTP request handlers, routing utilities, and response assembly' — RadialPlot fits none of those categories; CLAUDE.md even lists it as viz/RadialPlot.java, confirming a historical placement mismatch

GrammarConfigSerializer.java (config/ package) explicitly imports pl.marcinmilkowski.word_sketch.config.GrammarConfig and pl.marcinmilkowski.word_sketch.config.RelationConfig — same-package imports that Java does not require; no other file in the codebase does this

config/ package contains two sibling static-utility classes for RelationConfig: RelationPatternBuilder (named after the builder idiom but is a pure static util) and RelationUtils (named after the *Utils pattern used consistently by CqlUtils, LogDiceUtils, MathUtils); the naming divergence creates a minor style island inside config/

strengths: Domain-aligned package names (api, config, exploration, indexer, model, query, utils) let a new engineer explain every top-level package without reading its contents.
API layer is decomposed into five focused, package-private handler classes under a single public server class — ownership boundaries are predictable and the public surface is minimal.
Config package follows SRP consistently: GrammarConfig (data carrier), GrammarConfigLoader (file I/O), GrammarConfigSerializer (JSON output) each own exactly one responsibility.
model/exploration sub-package is well-justified and documented in model/package-info.java, making the two-tier model split (pure DTOs vs objects with derived accessors) explicit.
Every package has a package-info.java with a concise, accurate description — project docs and runtime reality are aligned.
high_level_elegance.issue_character: Issues are localized placement decisions rather than systemic decomposition failures: one computation class (RadialPlot) lives in the wrong package, and the model package has a minor structural asymmetry; neither obscures the overall domain structure.
high_level_elegance.score_rationale: The codebase scores above the 80 'solid but uneven' anchor on most high_level_elegance axes — ownership is clear, packages map to domain capabilities, and the API handler decomposition is consistent. It falls just below 80 because the api package blurs its own stated boundary by hosting RadialPlot (a pure visualization-computation concern), and the model/ layout has a mild asymmetry (flat word-sketch types alongside a sub-packaged exploration tier) that a new engineer must pause to reason about. These are genuine ownership-clarity gaps, not style nits, which pulls the score to 79.

strengths: Minimal, purposeful dependency list: only 6 production artifacts, each with a clear and fully utilised role.
No duplicate libraries for the same purpose — single JSON library (Jackson), single logging stack (SLF4J + Logback), no competing HTTP client libraries.
Jackson versions are pinned consistently using a shared ${jackson.version} property, eliminating version drift between jackson-core and jackson-databind.
jspecify annotation library is used widely and consistently rather than mixing multiple nullable-annotation libraries (e.g. javax, JetBrains, Checker).
Test-scoped dependencies (JUnit 5 jupiter-api, jupiter-engine, platform-launcher) are properly scoped and do not leak into production classpath.
dependency_health.issue_character: The single structural issue is one redundant explicit declaration (jackson-core alongside jackson-databind), plus an environmental procurement concern around BlackLab 4.x not being on Maven Central — neither is a code defect, both are low-severity.
dependency_health.score_rationale: This codebase scores well on dependency health: the dependency graph is intentionally lean, every artifact is justified by actual usage, and there are no competing libraries for the same concern. The score stops short of the high 80s primarily because jackson-core is declared redundantly (it is always transitively provided by jackson-databind), which adds a minor maintenance surface, and because the BlackLab 4.x artifacts require an out-of-band install step that is unusual friction for a Maven project. These are minor and cosmetic compared to the healthy overall picture.

strengths: Guard clauses and early returns applied uniformly across all public and package-private methods — null/empty checks are consistently first, reducing nesting.

Phase decomposition in CollocateQueryHelper and MultiSeedExplorer is clean and balanced: each private method has a single stated responsibility, named carrier records bridge phases without raw parallel collections.
Private record types used pervasively for local data structures (HitRecord, CollocateSearch, BcqlRequest, etc.) make method signatures intention-revealing rather than tuple-like.
State mutation is explicit and local throughout: accumulator maps are passed by reference with clear ownership, and no shared mutable state exists between pipeline stages.
Edge-case handling in BlackLabSnippetParser is precise and uniform — every method has the same null-guard idiom, and the safeParts/null-concordance fallback path is clean.
low_level_elegance.issue_character: The issues are isolated local-pattern inconsistencies rather than systemic design failures: one intra-class code duplication, one undocumented N-sequential-IO fan-out in a hot loop, and one shared-method abstraction that hides divergent caller semantics through inconsistent lambda policies.
low_level_elegance.score_rationale: The codebase is well above the 60 'significant drag' threshold — control flow is direct, phased decomposition is consistently applied, named carrier records prevent tuple anti-patterns, and null-guard conventions are uniform. The moderate pull-down comes from one concrete intra-class duplication (the corpus-frequency pre-fetch pattern copied verbatim between two private methods in CollocateQueryHelper), an undocumented N-IO loop in SemanticFieldExplorer that is locally invisible, and extractionlambda inconsistency that weakens the collectFrequenciesAndPosFromGroups abstraction. These are real but narrow issues that don't undermine the overall clean local structure, placing the score solidly in the 70-75 'solid but with identifiable local rough edges' band.

strengths: ExploreResponseAssembler centralises all model-to-presentation translation for exploration responses in one place, keeping model classes free of JSON concerns.
ExplorationService interface makes the HTTP-to-exploration seam explicit, testable, and stable; handlers never reach through to algorithm internals.
GrammarConfigLoader / GrammarConfig / GrammarConfigSerializer form a clean three-layer split: I/O, data, and serialization each own their concern with inward-only dependencies.
QueryExecutor default convenience methods keep BCQL pattern-construction out of HTTP handler code, avoiding duplicated RelationPatternBuilder calls at call sites.
HttpApiUtils.wrapWithErrorHandling provides a single, consistent error-propagation mapping point for all handler boundaries.
mid_level_elegance.issue_character: The issues are isolated rather than systemic: one concrete seam inconsistency (alias resolution absent at the concordance boundary) and two minor predicate-duplication cases; the overall boundary design is disciplined and the translation points are well-identified.
mid_level_elegance.score_rationale: The codebase scores solidly above 70 because the major boundaries — HTTP-to-exploration, model-to-JSON, config load/data/serialize — are explicit, minimal, and each translate in one obvious place. The score stops short of 85 primarily because of the alias-resolution gap between ConcordanceHandlers and ExplorationHandlers: a developer using identical 'relation' parameter values across sibling endpoints will see different behaviour with no warning, which is the defining failure mode of mid-level seam design. The predicate duplication in ComparisonResult is a secondary friction point that adds maintenance risk at the model boundary.

strengths: Guard clauses are consistently accompanied by explanatory comments that justify their presence (e.g. BlackLabSnippetParser position<1 note, CollocateQueryHelper OVER_FETCH_FACTOR comment), so defensive code never reads as noise

Control-flow phasing in SemanticFieldExplorer (fetchSeedCollocates, buildNounToCollocatesMap, filterByMinShared, identifyCoreCollocates) names each algorithmic phase explicitly, making the flow self-documenting

The extractCollocateLemma method in CollocateQueryHelper correctly handles the -1 sentinel from findLabelTokenIndex with an explicit > 0 guard and a named fallback, showing awareness of the failure mode
No dead code paths after unconditional returns, no always-true/always-false conditions, and no identical if/else branches were found across the 22 reviewed files
logic_clarity.issue_character: The issues are isolated, edge-case inconsistencies rather than systemic: one callsite (executeSurfacePattern) silently swallows a -1 sentinel that a sibling callsite (executeBcqlQuery) handles explicitly, and a single-value enum's isValid method duplicates a hardcoded string instead of deriving from the enum — both confined to one file each.
logic_clarity.score_rationale: The codebase is mostly clean from a logic-clarity perspective: guards are explained, algorithm phases are well-named, and no broad-pattern defects were found. What pulls the score below 80 is one genuine inconsistency — executeSurfacePattern silently produces empty results when findLabelTokenIndex returns -1, while executeBcqlQuery correctly handles the same sentinel — plus a false comment in BlackLabSnippetParser that misrepresents the sentinel value's contract. These are narrow but real: a developer relying on the comment or mirroring the executeSurfacePattern pattern in a new callsite could introduce a hard-to-spot silent failure. A solid 78 reflects code that does what it claims across the vast majority of paths, with a small cluster of low-severity but real logic-clarity gaps.

strengths: JSpecify @NonNull/@Nullable annotations applied consistently to public interface methods across QueryExecutor, HttpApiUtils, ExplorationService, and BlackLabSnippetParser
Sealed ConcordanceResult interface with explicit permits clause enforces exhaustive handling of result subtypes
Domain model uses well-typed records (WordSketchResult, CollocateResult, ExplorationResult) rather than untyped maps, keeping type contracts strong in the query and exploration layers
Optional<T> used appropriately in GrammarConfig.relation(String) for nullable-lookup semantics, with Javadoc explaining the design choice vs @Nullable
type_safety.issue_character: Issues are isolated to the HTTP presentation layer and amount to one enum-bypass pattern and the universal use of Map<String, Object> for JSON response assembly — neither affects the domain or query layers, which have strong type discipline.
type_safety.score_rationale: The codebase sits in the 75-80 band: the domain and query layers show exemplary Java type discipline — sealed types, JSpecify annotations on public APIs, well-typed records, and appropriate Optional usage — placing it above the 60 'significant drag' anchor. The main drag is the VisualizationHandlers RenderMode enum bypass (an enum is defined but validation uses a raw string literal and the type is never actually used for dispatch), plus the Map<String, Object> response pattern that erases all shape guarantees at the HTTP boundary. These are bounded to the presentation layer, keeping the overall score from reaching the 80+ 'solid but uneven' threshold.

strengths: Seven clearly-named top-level packages each with a package-info.java description make the top-level map easy to read for a new developer
model/exploration/ sub-package isolates exploration-specific types from shared model types, avoiding a flat model package that mixes concerns
Package-private visibility is applied consistently to internal helpers (RadialPlot, MultiSeedExplorer, CollocateProfileComparator), keeping accidental cross-package coupling low

Handler decomposition in api/ (SketchHandlers, ConcordanceHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, CorpusQueryHandlers, VisualizationHandlers) gives each HTTP concern its own class without scattering them across packages
structure_nav.issue_character: Placement outliers where a class's actual concern does not match its package's declared scope — SVG rendering in the HTTP handler package, JSON serialization in the config loading package — create small but real moments of 'why is this here?' for a developer navigating by package.
structure_nav.score_rationale: The codebase sits at the solid-but-uneven tier. A developer can predict where to find most things from the directory tree alone, and the consistent use of package-info files and package-private visibility reduces noise. However, RadialPlot (SVG engine) in api/ and GrammarConfigSerializer (presentation-layer JSON serializer) in config/ each contradict their package's stated purpose, producing the recurring friction characteristic of an 80 rather than a 90. Neither misplacement is severe enough to impede navigation significantly, but they represent the pattern of accepting boundary drift when a concern only has one caller.

strengths: Uniform error contract: every HTTP endpoint is wrapped by wrapWithErrorHandling, producing consistent 400/413/500 responses without per-handler boilerplate
Shared param-parsing utilities (requireParam, parseIntParam, parseDoubleParam) are used consistently across all handlers — no raw parse calls scattered through handler code
ExplorationService methods use typed options objects instead of long flat parameter lists, keeping method arity at 3 across all four service methods

buildSharedExploreResponse + ExploreParameterExtras/ExploreVariantFields provide an explicit, reusable envelope pattern that correctly unifies explore, explore-multi, and compare response shapes at the outer layer

Handler separation (SketchHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, ConcordanceHandlers, CorpusQueryHandlers, VisualizationHandlers) is consistent with route registration, giving each handler a coherent and limited responsibility
api_surface_coherence.issue_character: The issues are localized naming and shape inconsistencies at the seam between the service layer and the HTTP response layer — not architectural chaos, but visible enough that a client integrating all three exploration endpoints would encounter surprising differences.
api_surface_coherence.score_rationale: The API surface has strong mechanical consistency: error handling, param parsing, and options objects all follow clear patterns that a developer would quickly learn. What pulls the score down is a cluster of shape and naming divergences at the response-assembly level: the compare endpoint serializes 'adjectives' while explore uses 'seed_collocates', fetchExamples uses 'headword' while HTTP uses 'seed', and the two example-fetching endpoints emit slightly different response fields despite being conceptually parallel. These are real friction points for clients but do not reflect deep structural inconsistency. Against the global anchors this sits between 'solid but uneven' (80) and 'significant drag' (60), closer to the upper end because the foundation is coherent and the issues are confined to response-body naming rather than protocol or method-ordering problems.

strengths: Domain vocabulary is used with high consistency across all layers: 'collocate', 'logDice', 'bcql', 'seed', 'sketch', and 'lemma' never change meaning between packages.
Model record types (CollocateProfile, CoreCollocate, ComparisonResult, Edge) have precise, intention-revealing names that map directly to domain concepts with no generic placeholders.
Private implementation records like SeedCollocateData, AggregatedCollocateStats, HitRecord, and ExecutedSketch all communicate their role clearly without needing comments.

The exploration class hierarchy (SemanticFieldExplorer, MultiSeedExplorer, CollocateProfileComparator, ExplorationService) uses names that accurately describe each class's responsibility and their delegation relationship.
Handler method names in the API layer (handleConcordanceExamples, handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti, routeSketchRequest) are specific enough that their purpose is clear from the name alone.
naming_quality.issue_character: Naming issues are localized to two areas: the ExplorationHandlers class (ambiguous internal record names and a confusing shared-method name that clashes with domain vocabulary) and a handful of inconsistencies in how similar conversion/query methods are named across files.
naming_quality.score_rationale: The codebase shows strong naming discipline at the domain model and class level, with precise vocabulary and no generic verbs ('process', 'manage', 'do') anywhere in the production code. What pulls the score down from the 80s is a cluster of naming friction points in ExplorationHandlers (ExploreParameterExtras/ExploreVariantFields opacity, buildSharedExploreResponse domain-word collision) and a cross-cutting inconsistency in result-to-map conversion method names and the collocatesPerSeed/nouns_per parameter name mismatch. These are real developer friction points but are contained — a 75 reflects 'generally solid naming with noticeable rough patches in one subsystem and scattered inconsistencies elsewhere.'

strengths: Consistent StubQueryExecutor infrastructure reused across all handler and exploration tests, keeping tests fast, hermetic, and easy to read.
Handler tests (ExplorationHandlers, SketchHandlers, ConcordanceHandlers, CorpusQueryHandlers) cover both happy-path and all major validation/negative paths (400, 413) for every public endpoint.
WordSketchApiServerTest provides a genuine cross-module integration test: it starts the full HTTP server on an ephemeral port and verifies routing, health-check, and clean shutdown.
Core exploration logic (SemanticFieldExplorer, MultiSeedExplorer, CollocateProfileComparator) is well unit-tested with canned stub data and meaningful behavioural assertions.
Live-index and indexer tests are correctly gated behind @Disabled and Assumptions.assumeTrue, preventing false CI failures rather than masking them.
test_strategy.issue_character: The issues are structurally concentrated in one subsystem: the BlackLab integration layer (indexing pipeline and live-query execution) has zero CI-runnable tests, meaning every automated green build leaves the most complex, I/O-intensive paths entirely unvalidated.
test_strategy.score_rationale: Unit test coverage across the exploration, config, API, and utility layers is solid and well-structured — closer to the 80-range. However, the full indexing pipeline (BlackLabConllUIndexer) and all real BlackLab query execution (BlackLabQueryExecutorIntegrationTest, BlackLabQueryExecutorTest.LiveIndex) are permanently @Disabled in CI, so a developer making changes to those critical paths cannot rely on the test suite to catch regressions. This matches the 70-anchor: 'a developer would encounter enough inconsistency or friction that they can't fully trust patterns they've seen elsewhere' — the patterns they see in the well-covered layers do not transfer to the BlackLab subsystem. The score settles at 72 rather than lower because the disabled tests are clearly documented with instructions for local execution, and the unit layer genuinely covers the business logic above the BlackLab boundary.

strengths: Zero naming-convention drift: Java camelCase/PascalCase/UPPER_SNAKE_CASE is applied uniformly across every package with no exceptions
All packages have package-info.java files that document their mandates, making expected vs. actual placement easy to verify
Intentional type-strategy variation (record vs class) in model/exploration/ is explicitly documented with rationale in package-info.java — not an outlier
Handler siblings in api/ follow a consistent protocol: package-private class, constructor injection of dependencies, no shared interface required
Package hierarchy cleanly separates api, config, exploration, model, query, utils, and indexer with no observable scope leakage beyond the RadialPlot case
convention_outlier.issue_character: Isolated placement and import anomalies rather than systemic drift — one misplaced visualization class, one redundant-import file, and one naming-suffix inconsistency between two sibling utility classes in the same package.
convention_outlier.score_rationale: The codebase scores well above the midpoint for this dimension: naming conventions are flawless, package boundaries are well-documented, and the type-strategy variation is intentional and documented. The deduction comes from three specific, fixable outliers — RadialPlot in the wrong package, same-package imports in GrammarConfigSerializer, and an inconsistent *Builder vs *Utils suffix pair within config/ — none of which are systemic or pervasive.

issues.summary: RadialPlot (SVG layout computation) lives in api/, blurring that package's HTTP-handler responsibility
issues.suggestion: Move RadialPlot to a new top-level package (e.g. pl.marcinmilkowski.word_sketch.viz or pl.marcinmilkowski.word_sketch.render) or to model/ as a pure data-building class. VisualizationHandlers calls RadialPlot directly, so only that one call site needs updating. Update api/package-info.java to drop any reference to rendering if added.
evidence: RadialPlot.java contains pure SVG layout maths (spiral constants, guide circles, coordinate calculations) with no HTTP or JSON dependency — it is a rendering concern, not a handler concern.
api/package-info.java states the package owns 'HTTP request handlers, routing utilities, and response assembly'; SVG coordinate computation is none of these.
CLAUDE.md records a prior home of viz/RadialPlot.java, confirming the type was moved into api/ without a domain-alignment rationale.

issues.summary: model/ root mixes word-sketch types with exploration sub-package, leaving no sub-package for sketch results
issues.suggestion: Either (a) move QueryResults, PosGroup, RelationType into a model/sketch/ sub-package to create symmetric sub-packaging (exploration/ and sketch/ under model/), or (b) flatten model/exploration/* into model/ and update model/package-info.java to explain the single-level organization. Option (a) is preferable as the codebase grows. Update all import statements across api/, query/, and exploration/ accordingly.
evidence: model/exploration/ contains 10+ types (CollocateProfile, ComparisonResult, CoreCollocate, DiscoveredNoun, Edge, ExplorationOptions, ExplorationResult, FetchExamplesOptions, RelationEdgeType, SharingCategory, SingleSeedExplorationOptions) while model/ root holds 3 types (QueryResults, PosGroup, RelationType) that are equally domain-specific to the word-sketch feature.
A new engineer expecting parity would look for model/sketch/ (or similar) for word-sketch result types and not find it, forcing them to scan the flat root before understanding the asymmetry.
model/package-info.java documents the DTOs vs derived-accessor distinction but does not explain why sketch types are flat while exploration types are sub-packaged.

issues.summary: jackson-core declared explicitly in pom.xml despite being a mandatory transitive dependency of jackson-databind
issues.suggestion: Remove the explicit jackson-core dependency block from pom.xml. If a specific jackson-core version must be enforced (e.g. to override a transitive version), use a <dependencyManagement> entry instead of a compile-scope dependency declaration.
evidence: pom.xml lines 47-50 declare com.fasterxml.jackson.core:jackson-core:${jackson.version} as an explicit compile-scope dependency.
jackson-databind always pulls in jackson-core as a mandatory transitive dependency at the same version; the explicit entry is therefore redundant.

The three files that import com.fasterxml.jackson.core.JsonProcessingException (CorpusQueryHandlers.java, VisualizationHandlers.java, GrammarConfigLoader.java) would continue to compile correctly even if the explicit entry were removed, because jackson-databind is still present.

issues.summary: Identical corpus-frequency pre-fetch loop duplicated in buildAndRankCollocates and scoreHits within the same class
issues.suggestion: Extract a private helper method 'prefetchCorpusFrequencies(Collection<String> lemmas) throws IOException -> Map<String, Long>' that iterates over the lemma collection, calls getTotalFrequency, and returns the populated map. Replace both loops with a single call to this helper.
evidence: buildAndRankCollocates() lines: 'Map<String, Long> collocateCorpusFreqs = new HashMap<>(); for (String lemma : freqMap.keySet()) { collocateCorpusFreqs.put(lemma, getTotalFrequency(lemma)); }'

scoreHits() lines: 'Map<String, Long> collocateCorpusFreqs = new HashMap<>(); for (String collocate : collocateFreqMap.keySet()) { collocateCorpusFreqs.put(collocate, getTotalFrequency(collocate)); }' — structurally identical loop in the same class

issues.summary: buildNounToCollocatesMap issues up to topPredicates sequential I/O calls in a loop with no documentation of the fan-out cost
issues.suggestion: Add an inline comment above the loop explaining the fan-out: e.g. '// Issues up to topPredicates sequential index queries; capped by seedCollocateScores.size() which is bounded by opts.base().topCollocates()'. Optionally add a defensive cap: 'int limit = Math.min(seedCollocateScores.size(), topPredicates);' before the loop to make the bound explicit.
evidence: buildNounToCollocatesMap iterates 'for (String collocate : seedCollocateScores.keySet())' and calls 'executor.executeCollocations(collocate, ...)' inside the loop — with topPredicates defaulting to 20, this is 20 sequential index queries per exploration call.
No comment documents the N-query fan-out, no time budget or circuit-breaker is mentioned, and the caller (exploreByPattern) does not see the cost at its call site.

issues.summary: Three call sites of collectFrequenciesAndPosFromGroups pass inconsistent lambda extraction policies, obscuring different fallback semantics behind a shared method
issues.suggestion: Name and extract each extraction policy as a private static method or named constant (e.g. 'lemmaByXmlFallback', 'lastXmlLemma', 'collocateByPositionWithFallback') so call sites document the policy in use. Alternatively, add a brief comment at each call site indicating which extraction strategy is intentional and why it differs.
evidence: executeCollocations() passes 'identity -> { String collocateLemma = BlackLabSnippetParser.extractLemmaWithFallback(identity); return collocateLemma.isEmpty() ? null : collocateLemma; }' — uses extractLemmaWithFallback (which itself has a plain-text fallback).
queryAndRankDepCollocates() passes 'BlackLabSnippetParser::extractLastLemma' — no fallback, returns null on miss.
executeSurfacePattern() passes a two-tier lambda: extractCollocateFromXmlByPosition first, then extractPlainTextTokenAt — a different fallback chain not present in the other two callers.

The shared method's null-skip ('if (lemma == null || lemma.isEmpty()) continue') normalises different missing-lemma policies, making the per-caller semantics invisible from the collectFrequenciesAndPosFromGroups signature.

issues.summary: ConcordanceHandlers skips RelationUtils.resolveRelationAlias, so short aliases (e.g. 'adj_predicate') silently fail there but work on sibling explore endpoints
issues.suggestion: Extract alias resolution into a shared helper used by both handlers: add a package-private static method resolveRelation(Map<String,String> params, String defaultId) in HttpApiUtils or RelationUtils that applies resolveRelationAlias before the GrammarConfig lookup, and call it from both parseConcordanceExamplesRequest and resolveRelationConfig. This collapses the two divergent paths into one.
evidence: ExplorationHandlers.resolveRelationConfig calls RelationUtils.resolveRelationAlias(params.getOrDefault("relation", "noun_adj_predicates")) before grammarConfig.relation(relationId).

ConcordanceHandlers.parseConcordanceExamplesRequest stores params.getOrDefault("relation", "noun_adj_predicates") raw and passes it directly to grammarConfig.relation(req.relation()) with no alias resolution.

A caller passing relation=adj_predicate to /api/concordance/examples gets rel.isEmpty()==true and falls back to a proximity pattern (logged as a warning), while the same parameter to /api/semantic-field/explore resolves correctly to 'noun_adj_predicates'.

issues.summary: ComparisonResult.fullyShared/partiallyShared/specific() re-implement filter predicates already encapsulated in CollocateProfile, creating a dual-definition boundary seam
issues.suggestion: Rewrite the three stream accessors to delegate to CollocateProfile's own predicates: fullyShared() -> collocates.stream().filter(CollocateProfile::isFullyShared), partiallyShared() -> filter(CollocateProfile::isPartiallyShared), specific() -> filter(CollocateProfile::isSpecific). This makes ComparisonResult consistent with summaryCounts() and ensures predicate changes propagate from one place.
evidence: ComparisonResult.fullyShared() filters by a.presentInCount() == nouns.size(); CollocateProfile.isFullyShared() checks presentInCount == totalNouns — same predicate expressed twice.
ComparisonResult.partiallyShared() repeats a.presentInCount() >= 2 && a.presentInCount() < nouns.size(); CollocateProfile.isPartiallyShared() already encapsulates this.
ComparisonResult.summaryCounts() correctly delegates to c.sharingCategory(), so the same object has both the delegating and non-delegating paths; the stream accessors don't follow the same pattern.

issues.summary: executeSurfacePattern silently drops all groups when findLabelTokenIndex returns -1 (label 2: absent), while the sibling executeBcqlQuery path handles the same sentinel with an explicit fallback
issues.suggestion: Add an explicit check in executeSurfacePattern after CqlUtils.findLabelTokenIndex: if collocatePos < 1, either log a debug/warn message and return early with an empty list, or mirror extractCollocateLemma's fallback (collocatePos > 0 guard + extractLastLemma fallback) in the lambda. Aligning both callsites removes the silent failure mode and makes the -1 case visible.
evidence: CqlUtils.findLabelTokenIndex returns -1 when the label is not found (line 'return -1;' in both early-exit and fall-through paths)
In CollocateQueryHelper.collectHits, collocatePos is passed to extractCollocateLemma which guards with 'if (collocatePos > 0)' and falls back to extractLastLemma — -1 is handled gracefully

In BlackLabQueryExecutor.executeSurfacePattern, collocatePos is passed directly into the lambda: extractCollocateFromXmlByPosition(identity, collocatePos) then extractPlainTextTokenAt(identity, collocatePos); both methods guard 'position < 1' and return null, causing every group to be skipped with no warning log
The two callsites exhibit different behavior for the identical -1 sentinel, making the logic of executeSurfacePattern inconsistent with the established pattern

issues.summary: BlackLabSnippetParser comment claims findLabelTokenIndex 'returns >= 1', but the method returns -1 on failure, creating a false contract that misleads future callers
issues.suggestion: Update the comment to accurately reflect the actual sentinel: 'CqlUtils.findLabelTokenIndex returns -1 when the label is absent; callers must check position > 0 before calling this method.' Also update the stale class reference from BcqlPatternUtils to CqlUtils.
evidence: BlackLabSnippetParser.extractCollocateFromXmlByPosition comment: 'all current callers pass values from BcqlPatternUtils.findLabelTokenIndex() which returns >= 1, or explicitly check > 0 before calling'
CqlUtils.findLabelTokenIndex (the successor to BcqlPatternUtils) returns -1 in two places: when pattern is null, and when the label is not found
The comment's first clause 'returns >= 1' is false for the not-found case; the class name 'BcqlPatternUtils' is stale (the method now lives in CqlUtils)
A developer reading the comment could incorrectly conclude that no > 0 guard is needed when calling extractCollocateFromXmlByPosition in a new callsite

issues.summary: VisualizationHandlers.RenderMode.isValid hardcodes the literal string 'SIGNED' instead of deriving from enum values, so adding a new enum constant would silently bypass validation
issues.suggestion: Replace the hardcoded string check with an enum-based lookup: try { RenderMode.valueOf(value.toUpperCase()); return true; } catch (IllegalArgumentException e) { return false; } — or use Arrays.stream(RenderMode.values()).anyMatch(m -> m.name().equalsIgnoreCase(value)). Either approach keeps isValid automatically in sync with the enum's defined constants.
evidence: enum RenderMode { SIGNED; static boolean isValid(String value) { return "SIGNED".equalsIgnoreCase(value); } }
The isValid method implies 'validates against the defined enum modes' but actually only validates against a single hardcoded string literal
If a second mode (e.g. UNSIGNED) is added to the enum, isValid will continue to reject it unless manually updated — the enum definition and the validation are decoupled with no compile-time linkage

issues.summary: RenderMode enum defined but bypassed: validation uses a raw string literal and the type is never used for dispatch
issues.suggestion: Replace the isValid helper with an Arrays.stream(RenderMode.values()).anyMatch(m -> m.name().equalsIgnoreCase(value)) check, or use a try/catch around RenderMode.valueOf(value.toUpperCase()). Convert the validated string to RenderMode before passing it to RadialPlot so the downstream API accepts RenderMode rather than String.
evidence: VisualizationHandlers defines 'private enum RenderMode { SIGNED; static boolean isValid(String value) { return "SIGNED".equalsIgnoreCase(value); } }' — the enum constant is not used in the isValid body
Adding a second mode to the enum (e.g. GRADIENT) would require manually updating the hard-coded string in isValid; the enum's compile-time completeness guarantee is never exercised

The validated mode string is passed as raw String to RadialPlot.renderFromItems(center, items, width, height, mode) rather than converted to RenderMode, so the enum never participates in downstream dispatch

issues.summary: RadialPlot SVG renderer lives in the api/ package whose stated purpose is HTTP handlers and routing
issues.suggestion: Move RadialPlot.java to a new api/viz/ sub-package (or a top-level viz/ package) so the api/ package contains only HTTP-protocol classes; update the package-info to reflect the narrower scope. Since RadialPlot is package-private, widen its access to package-private in the new package and update VisualizationHandlers to import from the new location.
evidence: api/package-info.java declares the package purpose as 'HTTP request handlers, routing utilities, and response assembly'
RadialPlot.java is a 200-line SVG generation class with spiral-layout maths, canvas scaling, and XML escaping — none of which are HTTP handler concerns
RadialPlot is package-private and consumed only by VisualizationHandlers, so moving it would require no public-API changes

issues.summary: GrammarConfigSerializer (a Jackson-backed JSON presenter) lives in config/ whose concern is configuration loading and data
issues.suggestion: Move GrammarConfigSerializer to api/ (as a package-private class used only by SketchHandlers) or to a dedicated config/serialization/ sub-package. This removes Jackson from the config package's dependency surface and makes the config package a pure data-loading module. Tests that reference GrammarConfigSerializer should move to the api test package alongside it.
evidence: GrammarConfigSerializer.java Javadoc says it produces ObjectNode 'for API responses and diagnostic output' — a presentation concern
GrammarConfigSerializer introduces a Jackson ObjectMapper static field into the config package, which otherwise depends only on JDK and jspecify
config/package-info.java lists 'serialization' as a concern to acknowledge the mismatch, but the class is a JSON presenter, not a configuration concern
The class is public and imported by test code in the config test package, making it an exposed API of the config package despite serving the HTTP layer

issues.summary: compare endpoint response uses 'adjectives' key while explore endpoints use 'seed_collocates', making the API surface inconsistent for a grammatical-relation-agnostic system
issues.suggestion: Rename the 'adjectives' / 'adjectives_count' keys in populateComparisonResponse to 'collocates' / 'collocates_count' to match the neutral vocabulary used throughout the rest of the exploration API. Update any frontend consumers accordingly.
evidence: populateComparisonResponse puts 'adjectives' and 'adjectives_count' into the response map (ExploreResponseAssembler.java)
populateExploreResponse puts 'seed_collocates', 'discovered_nouns', 'core_collocates' — no 'adjectives' key anywhere in explore responses
The system is explicitly grammatical-relation-agnostic (compare works across all relations), yet 'adjectives' as a key name hardcodes the adj_predicate use case into the public API response shape

issues.summary: ExplorationService.fetchExamples uses 'headword' as the parameter name but every HTTP handler and query parameter uses 'seed', creating a vocabulary mismatch at the service boundary
issues.suggestion: Rename the 'headword' parameter in ExplorationService.fetchExamples (and its implementation in SemanticFieldExplorer) to 'seed' to align with the vocabulary used everywhere else in the exploration API.
evidence: ExplorationService.fetchExamples signature: fetchExamples(@NonNull String collocate, @NonNull String headword, @NonNull RelationConfig relationConfig, @NonNull FetchExamplesOptions opts)
handleSemanticFieldExamples in ExplorationHandlers: String seed = requireParam(params, "seed"); ... semanticFieldExplorer.fetchExamples(collocate, seed, resolvedConfig, ...)
All other exploration methods use 'seed' / 'seeds' consistently in both interface and HTTP layer

issues.summary: The two concordance-examples endpoints (/api/semantic-field/examples and /api/concordance/examples) accept identical parameters but return different response fields, making them harder to use interchangeably
issues.suggestion: Align the two endpoints' response shapes: add 'total_results' to the concordance examples response and define whether 'fallback' belongs in both. Extract a shared response builder (similar to how ExploreResponseAssembler.collocateToExampleMap is already shared) to enforce the common shape.
evidence: handleSemanticFieldExamples response includes 'total_results' field but no 'fallback' field
handleConcordanceExamples response includes a 'fallback: true' field (when fallback logic fires) but no 'total_results' field
Both endpoints accept the same query parameters: seed, collocate, relation, top — the differing response shapes are not signalled by any API documentation at the HTTP layer

issues.summary: ExploreParameterExtras and ExploreVariantFields have names that do not communicate their structural role in the response envelope
issues.suggestion: Rename ExploreParameterExtras to ExtraQueryParameters (or AdditionalQueryParams) and ExploreVariantFields to TopLevelResponseFields (or ResponseEnvelopeFields) so that each name reveals where its contents appear in the serialized JSON response.
evidence: ExploreParameterExtras (line 442) carries fields that land inside the 'parameters' sub-map of the response; ExploreVariantFields (line 452) carries fields that land at the top level of the response envelope.
The distinction is only discoverable via the Javadoc on buildSharedExploreResponse (lines 411–418); the names themselves give no hint about the structural difference.
'Extras' implies optional add-ons; 'VariantFields' implies schema variation — neither name communicates the JSON path where the fields are placed.

issues.summary: buildSharedExploreResponse uses 'shared' as an implementation concept that clashes with 'shared' as a core domain term
issues.suggestion: Rename to buildExploreResponseEnvelope or buildCommonExploreResponse (using 'common' rather than 'shared') to avoid collision with the domain vocabulary.
evidence: The method name 'buildSharedExploreResponse' (line 419) uses 'shared' to mean 'reused across three handler methods' (handleSemanticFieldExplore, handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti, handleSemanticFieldComparison).

The same class and its collaborators use 'shared' as a domain term: CollocateProfile.isFullyShared(), ComparisonResult.fullyShared(), ComparisonResult.partiallyShared(), SharingCategory.FULLY_SHARED — all within the same conceptual layer.
A developer reading 'buildSharedExploreResponse' in context naturally interprets 'shared' as the domain concept (collocates shared across seeds), not as a method-reuse qualifier.

issues.summary: executeCollocateSearchImpl uses an 'Impl' suffix to encode a meaningful behavioral distinction that belongs in the method name
issues.suggestion: Rename executeCollocateSearchImpl to executeCollocateSearchCore or inline it, since the boolean parameter already makes the distinction explicit. If kept separate, name it executeCollocateSearchWithFlag(boolean withStoredHits) or similar.
evidence: CollocateQueryHelper exposes three methods: executeCollocateSearch, executeCollocateSearchWithContent, and executeCollocateSearchImpl.
The 'Impl' variant is the private delegate that accepts a boolean 'withStoredHits' flag — the flag determines whether hits are materialized for content retrieval.
'Impl' communicates nothing about behavior; the actual distinction (with/without stored hits) is already encoded in the names of the two public callers.

issues.summary: Java variable 'collocatesPerSeed' and HTTP parameter 'nouns_per' name different quantities for the same value
issues.suggestion: Align the names: either rename the HTTP parameter to 'collocates_per' (or 'top_per_seed') to match the Java variable's meaning, or rename the Java variable to 'nounsPerRelationStep' if the parameter really does control noun retrieval depth. Whichever is semantically correct, the parameter and variable should communicate the same quantity.
evidence: ExplorationHandlers line 262: int collocatesPerSeed = HttpApiUtils.parseIntParam(params, "nouns_per", 30);
'nouns_per' implies count of nouns per adjective (or per relation step); 'collocatesPerSeed' implies count of collocates fetched per seed word — these are different conceptual quantities.
The mismatch means an API consumer looking at the parameter name 'nouns_per' and a developer reading the Java variable 'collocatesPerSeed' form different mental models of what the parameter controls.

issues.summary: Four methods that convert result objects to JSON-ready maps use four incompatible naming conventions
issues.suggestion: Adopt a single convention for result-to-map serialization helpers, e.g. always use 'toMap' suffix (collocateResultToMap, wordSketchResultToMap) or always use 'serialize' prefix. The convention used in ExploreResponseAssembler (collocateToFullResultMap, collocateToExampleMap) is the most specific and should be the model.
evidence: ConcordanceHandlers: private static Map<String, Object> toResultMap(CollocateResult r)
SketchHandlers: private static Map<String, Object> formatWordSketchResult(WordSketchResult result)
ExploreResponseAssembler: public static Map<String, Object> collocateToFullResultMap(CollocateResult r)
ExploreResponseAssembler: public static Map<String, Object> collocateToExampleMap(CollocateResult r)
All four methods convert a domain result object to a Map<String, Object> for JSON serialization, but use to*, format*, and *ToMap* prefixes inconsistently.

issues.summary: Indexing pipeline and live-query paths are entirely @Disabled — zero CI coverage for the core BlackLab subsystem
issues.suggestion: Add a CI-runnable integration test tier using a small in-memory or temporary BlackLab index built from the existing src/test/resources/test-corpus.conllu. BlackLab supports in-memory index creation; use it in a @TempDir test to index the test corpus and run a handful of executeCollocations and executeSurfacePattern queries, removing the @Disabled on at least the ConlluConverter→indexer→query round-trip. This validates the entire pipeline without requiring an external index.
evidence: BlackLabConllUIndexerTest is class-level @Disabled; all five end-to-end indexing tests (document count, token count, missing-file IOException, constructor directory creation) never run in CI.
BlackLabQueryExecutorIntegrationTest is @Disabled; executeCollocations, executeSurfacePattern, getTotalFrequency against a real index are untested automatically.
BlackLabQueryExecutorTest.LiveIndex nested class is @Disabled; the only CI-runnable tests in that file are static helper tests for buildBcqlWithLemmaSubstitution — not query execution.

issues.summary: exploreMultiSeed_returnsNonNullResult uses only assertNotNull — provides no behavioural verification of the multi-seed path
issues.suggestion: Replace the assertNotNull-only test with assertions on the result's structure: verify that result.seeds() contains the two input seeds, that result.seedCollocates() is non-null and non-empty given the stub data, and that result.coreCollocates() is empty when the stubs share no collocates. This turns the smoke test into a contract test for the multi-seed output shape.
evidence: The test body is: `assertNotNull(result, "Result should not be null")` — this passes as long as exploreMultiSeed does not return null or throw, regardless of whether the result is correctly populated.

The immediately following test (exploreMultiSeed_sharedCollocateInSeedMap) is more meaningful but tests only one property; the dedicated non-null test adds no coverage beyond what the second test already implies.

issues.summary: RadialPlot (SVG renderer) lives in api/ whose stated mandate is HTTP handlers and response assembly
issues.suggestion: Create a src/main/java/pl/marcinmilkowski/word_sketch/viz/ (or rendering/) package, move RadialPlot there, change its access modifier from package-private to package-accessible (or public), and update VisualizationHandlers to import it. Update package-info.java in the new package and remove RadialPlot from api/.
evidence: api/package-info.java: 'HTTP request handlers, routing utilities, and response assembly for the REST API' — RadialPlot does none of these
RadialPlot is a self-contained SVG layout engine; it has no HttpExchange parameter, no HTTP concern, and is only coupled to VisualizationHandlers via package-private access
CLAUDE.md documents the class at viz/RadialPlot.java, confirming an intended (or prior) placement in a rendering/viz package that was never implemented or was lost during refactoring

issues.summary: GrammarConfigSerializer imports same-package classes GrammarConfig and RelationConfig unnecessarily
issues.suggestion: Remove the two redundant same-package import lines from GrammarConfigSerializer.java; the classes are visible without import.
evidence: Lines 4-5: 'import pl.marcinmilkowski.word_sketch.config.GrammarConfig;' and 'import pl.marcinmilkowski.word_sketch.config.RelationConfig;' — both classes are in the same package (config/) and need no import statement in Java
No other file in the codebase imports classes from its own package; this is an isolated style outlier within config/

issues.summary: config/ contains two sibling static utility classes for RelationConfig named with inconsistent suffixes: RelationPatternBuilder (*Builder) vs RelationUtils (*Utils)
issues.suggestion: Rename RelationPatternBuilder to RelationPatternUtils (or merge its methods into RelationUtils under a 'pattern building' section) to align with the *Utils naming convention used throughout the codebase. Update all references in GrammarConfigLoader, ConcordanceHandlers, SketchHandlers, and SemanticFieldExplorer.
evidence: Both are 'public final class' with 'private constructor()' and only static methods — the canonical Java static-utility shape; the codebase names three other static-utility classes with the *Utils suffix: CqlUtils, LogDiceUtils, MathUtils

RelationPatternBuilder is not a mutable builder (no fluent setters, no build() call); the *Builder suffix is therefore misleading relative to the GoF Builder pattern and diverges from the *Utils convention used for all peer utilities

RelationConfig.java Javadoc acknowledges the split: 'Pattern-building and POS-group inference logic lives in RelationPatternBuilder' — the naming reflects a logical split but uses an inconsistent label
